# Tempo and mode of evolution from comparative data. {#tempo-mode}

The emphasis in the literature is often on using comparative methods to “fix” statistical tests so that p-values are valid (see Chapter 3). For the past 15 years, however, the cutting-edge of methods development has been on using comparative methods to infer the nature of the evolutionary process: evolution is the reason that we see phylogenetic signal in comparative data, so it makes sense that we should be able to use phylogenies and comparative data to infer the evolutionary process. Here we distinguish the mode (i.e. models of evolution, with discrete and continuous data) and tempo (i.e. rates of evolution) of evolution. The issues we will cover include:

•	More complex Brownian models. 
•	Non-Brownian trait evolution.
●	The SLOUCH family of models
•	Speciational evolution.
•	Clade dependent rates.
•	Trait dependent rates.
•	Species-interactions.

As far as possible we will explain these in the same framework as Chapter 3: i.e. emphasising that a relatively simple statistical framework/understanding is the stepping-off point for understanding a wide range of different models. This overlaps with a recent text (Harmon 2018): however, our target audience will be different, and we see that text as a next step.
